nativeRange.habitat: Large, slow-moving rivers, floodplain lakes, billabongs, and swamps in tropical northern Australia and southern New Guinea; calm or sluggish backwaters near the surface with overhanging vegetation

decorPreferences: large open swimming area at the surface
large smooth rocks or driftwood at edges for territory definition
dim to moderate lighting to reduce stress
surface cover of floating plants optional

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in the wild; consumes fish, frogs, large insects, small birds, and other surface prey. In captivity, condition on large feeder fish initially, then wean onto market prawns, whole fish (smelt, whitebait), large earthworms, and high-quality carnivore sticks or pellets. Live prey can be weaned off with persistence. Feed 2–3 times per week for adults to avoid obesity; juveniles fed daily. Remove uneaten food promptly — water quality degrades quickly in a large tank.

avoidWith: all other fish in most cases (piscivore — will eat any tankmate that fits in its mouth)
other arowanas (intense territorial aggression between conspecifics)
mid-water or surface fish of any size (ambush risk or harassment)

imageCandidates.notes: Lateral view of adult specimen showing characteristic pearlescent scale margins and barbels. Verify resolution before use.

summary: The Jardini Arowana (Northern Saratoga) is a large, predatory Australian-endemic osteoglossid reaching 90 cm. An obligate air-breather and surface ambush piscivore, it demands a species-only tank of 1500 L or more. Specialist-only species due to extreme size, aggression, and specialist needs.
careNotes: The Jardini Arowana (Scleropages jardinii) is one of two Australian Saratoga species — distinguished from the Southern Saratoga (S. leichardti) by its more northerly range (Queensland’s Gulf Country, Northern Territory, and southern Papua New Guinea), larger adult size, and the pearlescent crescent markings on each scale. Like all osteoglossids, it is an obligate air-breather with a modified swim bladder that functions as a primitive lung; it must have continuous unobstructed access to the surface — never block airspace above the water. Reaching up to 90 cm and displaying intense territorial aggression, this species is realistically suitable only as a single specimen in a purpose-built tank of 1500 L or more with a footprint of at least 300 cm × 100 cm. A heavy, tightly fitting lid with no gaps is non-negotiable — jardinii are powerful, athletic jumpers that will launch themselves out of any opening. Water quality must be pristine: large bioload from carnivore feeding demands robust biological filtration with high turnover, and large weekly water changes (30–50%) are standard. Temperature should be maintained in the warm tropical range (24–30°C); soft to moderately hard, near-neutral to slightly acidic water is preferred. Avoid bright lighting; subdued light reduces stress-related surface striking. Note: Australian export restrictions mean wild-caught specimens are not legally exported; the trade is supplied by captive-bred stock from Australia, Papua New Guinea, and licensed breeders. Not subject to CITES Appendix I listing (unlike Asian Arowana, S. formosus — fw-fish-143), but Australian wildlife export law applies. Common name ‘Northern Saratoga’ distinguishes it clearly from ‘Southern Saratoga’ (S. leichardti). Note correct spelling: jardinii (double-i), named after explorer F.L. Jardine.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder, paralleling the Asian Arowana (S. formosus — fw-fish-143). After spawning, the male incubates large yolky eggs (up to 200 per clutch) orally for approximately 6–7 weeks, then continues to brood free-swimming fry until the yolk sac is absorbed. Captive breeding is extremely rare due to the immense space required for a compatible pair and the intense aggression of both sexes. A minimum 3000 L purpose-built breeding pond or enclosure is advised. Sexing is difficult externally; females are generally larger with a broader girth when gravid. Breeding has been achieved in large specialist facilities and public aquaria. Fry are large at release and can take whole small fish and earthworms within days.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Scleropages jardinii
primary.notes: Primary source for taxonomy, water parameters, native range, adult size, diet, temperament, and aquarium requirements. Confirms paternal mouthbrooding, surface air-breathing, 90 cm adult size, and species-only recommendation.

additional.name: FishBase — Scleropages jardinii
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 90 cm TL), native range (Queensland, Northern Territory, southern New Guinea), taxonomy (Osteoglossidae, Osteoglossiformes), temperature and pH ranges, diet (carnivore/piscivore), IUCN status, and depth preference.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Arowana Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary trade/hobby guidance on arowana care applicable to jardinii: tank size requirements, lid security for jumping behaviour, feeding transition from live prey to prepared foods, and water quality management for large carnivores.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Arowana Species Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-focused coverage of Scleropages species including jardinii: distinguishes Northern Saratoga (S. jardinii) from Southern Saratoga (S. leichardti), covers Australian export regulations, confirms paternal mouthbrooding behaviour, and discusses captive breeding rarity.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Saratoga: Australia’s Native Arowana
additional.notes: Species account covering biology of both Australian Saratoga species; confirms jardinii distribution in northern Australia and PNG, obligate air-breathing, aggressive piscivore behaviour, and specialist-only suitability for home aquaria.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates up to ~200 large eggs orally for 6–7 weeks, continuing to brood free-swimming fry post-hatch. Captive breeding is exceptionally rare outside large specialist facilities; requires enormous enclosures and compatible, non-lethal pairing.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 90cm; aggressive piscivore needing 1500L+ species-only tank. Air-breather. Australia/PNG endemic; not as legally restricted as Asian Arowana (S. formosus) but Australian export restrictions apply.
